terms.definition: A deny permission that cannot be overridden by any other access control rule, including ad hoc rules and user-specific permissions. Only removal of the absolute deny policy itself can restore access.
A defined set of permissions generated by evaluating and combining all applicable access control rules for a specific object type, state, and domain.
Object-level permissions that can only grant access (not deny). Applied through lifecycle templates or the Edit Access Control interface for individual objects.
A domain that every access-controlled object in Windchill is associated with, either directly or indirectly. Controls access control, indexing, and notification rules for objects.
A workflow-driven exception to a security label at the site, organization, or sub-context level. Agreements are lifecycle-controlled objects that provide temporary access to security-labeled content.
A storage container for Windchill objects that is not visible to regular users. Every context has Default and System cabinets, and each user has a personal cabinet.
A permission that enables a user to move an object out of a context. Required when moving objects between different products, libraries, or projects.
A permission that enables a user to move an object out of an administrative domain. Required when a move operation changes the object's domain.
The domain automatically created and associated with a context (product, library, project, program) that governs data objects within that context.
Access control, indexing, or notification rules applied to a storage context. Domain rules apply to all objects of the same class within the domain and inherit from parent domains.
A custom domain manually created and associated with a specific folder to apply different access control policies than the parent context's default domain.
An access control technique where a deny rule is applied to 'all except' a selected participant, effectively granting exclusive access to a specific group while denying everyone else.
A domain-based rule that automatically sends email notifications to specified participants when certain system events occur on objects of a specific type.
A domain-based rule that identifies which objects to index and when, enabling keyword searches against object attributes and content files via the indexing server.
The organization-level domain that is the parent to all public Product and Library default domains. Enables setting access rules that govern all public PDM data for an organization.
The Windchill administrative utility used to manage domains, access control rules, indexing rules, and notification rules. Accessed via the Utilities section of a context.
A participant in an access control rule. Can be a user, group, organization, context team role, organization role, or pseudo-role (All or Owner).
A domain at the organization level that bypasses global rules from the Default and PDM domains, enabling restrictive access rules for private products, libraries, or projects.
A special role that refers to all Windchill users. Can be used to grant or deny permissions to everyone in the system.

A special role representing the creator of an object (by default). Permissions can only be granted to this role, never denied. Results in very strict rules superseding everything except absolute deny.
A classification placed on a Windchill object that denies all access to non-authorized participants. The strongest rule in the access control scheme, overriding all domain and ad hoc rules.
A domain created when a shared team is defined. Products or libraries using the shared team have their Default domain connected to this domain instead of the PDM domain.
A domain associated with the System cabinet that controls administrative templates such as lifecycle templates, workflow templates, and team templates.
